data.title: Equifax Q1 Preview: Management's Thoughts On Outlook Should Be Interesting
data.description:
Introduction

Equifax Inc. (NYSE:EFX) is due to report its Q1 ’25 results on April 22nd, so I wanted to go through some numbers and what I am going to be looking out for in the upcoming conference call to gauge the overall situation regarding the economy and the company’s performance.

What To Expect

Analysts expect the company to make around $1.41, and $1.00 of adjusted and GAAP EPS respectively, on $1.42B in revenues. The sales number translates to around 2% growth y/y, as well as essentially flat sequentially. The upcoming results show a lack of top-line growth, which tells us that the markets like the mortgage and hiring are coming in rather weak, leading to a lackluster top-line performance. The U.S. mortgage market is a significant headwind for the company, which has been affected by the overall higher interest rates.

So, how do the analysts’ estimates stack up against what the management guided back in Q4 ’24? The management forecast sales to be in the range of $1.39B to $1.42B, which is $1.405B at the midpoint, and slightly below the consensus. Adjusted EPS guidance given by the management is $1.33 to $1.43, which is $1.38 at the midpoint and below the consensus as well.

How likely is the company to miss or beat the consensus? In the last 10 quarters, the company beat EPS estimates 90% of the time, while it missed revenues 50% of the time. Given the slight differences in the consensus and management’s estimates, I wouldn’t be surprised to see a miss.

It is also worth noting that there have been 16 downward revisions to the company’s EPS, which further adds to the uncertainty of the numbers.

Overall, I don’t expect a blowout quarter, and I expect some misses here and there. What will matter the most is what the company going to say about the outlook going forward.

What I’m Looking For

Speaking of the outlook, I will be monitoring the reported decline in U.S. mortgage hard credit inquiries. EFX projected inquiry volumes to fall 12% y/y, so in the upcoming conference call, I will be looking out for any signs of this trend playing out, which aligns with the company’s expected outlook. In Q4 ‘24, we saw that despite headwinds, mortgage revenue growth in the U.S. came in at a whopping 29%, signaling that the demand is there. However, that was in the last three months of the year, so I will be looking to see how this segment performed in the last three months. The mortgage rates started to climb back up from the end of December, topping out at a little over 7% in mid-January. The next couple of months saw a decent decline in rates, however, since the Liberation Day tariff announcements, we saw a spike in rates once again. So, it will be interesting to hear what the company has to say about the interest rate environment, and whether the consumer is still resilient when interest rates are coming back up.

Moving on to Workforce Solutions, the company expects the segment to continue to face headwinds in 2025, with an 8% projected decline in the U.S. hiring activity. The upcoming quarter will give us a better idea of the trends in verification services, which were up 10% in Q4, and Talent solutions, which were down 9% in the same quarter. The workforce solutions seem to be the weak point going forward for the company, and any further deterioration in this segment doesn’t bode well for the economy overall.

In terms of the international outlook, Brazil performed quite well in the recent past, so I would like to see this sustained going forward. However, because of the global economic uncertainty currently, we may see some weakness in the LatAm and European regions.

In the previous quarter, the company guided 2025 as below:

I wonder if the company will adjust this guidance in the upcoming quarter or the following one due to uncertain markets globally caused by a tariff war, which could potentially lead to many economies going into a recession, leading to a decrease in the performance of the company. The only way I could see the company adjusting the above guidance is to the downside. There is zero chance of an increase in guidance in my opinion. Everyone wants to be conservative in these situations and being cautious in this environment is the right way to approach the situation.

Stabilization in mortgage rates as well as in hiring activity would be a positive signal for the company, however, with the current macroeconomic uncertainty that is not going to happen any time soon.

Closing Comments

I don’t see a reason to buy the company before the earnings announcements. The company’s lackluster top-line growth is not very appealing to me, and even after dropping around 20% from recent highs, the company is still trading at a forward multiple of 29. That is not enticing at all. The company would need to improve its top line or efficiency and profitability much more before I would consider it a good investment. And especially during these uncertain times when we have no idea what is going to happen in the next few months.

I would like to hear the management’s opinion on the U.S. and global markets and what kind of trends are they seeing to help us investors gauge the situation in the short term.

data.title: Knorr-Bremse Stock: Glad I Sold Some Before The Dip, Looking At 2025
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

This article is going to be about an update for Knorr-Bremse AG (OTCPK:KNRRY), a braking system company that I have been reviewing and investing in for about a year. I will make the case that if you use the tenets of valuation that I preach, there was a time when you could get out and make an excellent RoR not that long ago. That being said, I only sold 30% of my stake at the time. Because while the company's price was impressive, it was not at the level that's typically enough for me.

So why did I sell?

I did a number of sales and rotations at the time in January/February that had to do with getting out of cyclical industrial or dollar-denominated stocks. My general view was that President Trump's policies were initially not going to be good for the global economy and/or for the USD. Remember that I invest in base currency SEK, which in January had an exchange rate of 11.05. Now, we're in April, and the dollar has weakened to under 9.9. This is drop in a historically short period of time, and my sales were as such, excellently timed. With the money I earned, I bought shares in EUR-denominated companies, as well as NOK, SEK, DKK, GBP, and YPN.

In fact, some of the sales I have made are to be bought back in Knorr-Bremse shares going forward.

And here, using the 2024 results and the forecast, I am going to show you why I'll be investing more in the company and why I view it as a positive investment.

My latest article on the company can be found here.

Knorr Bremse and its future upside, following a 10% drop in February of 2025

So, as I said, the company had quite a nice little bounce in February. For some time, it traded above a native price target, or PT, of €92/share, which was very close to my PT. Because such a short-term movement was cause for notice, I actually went ahead and sold part of my position. Unfortunately, I did not sell enough. I am not one of the investors or analysts that make excuses or say that it doesn't matter because, in the long term, it will go up again (even though I believe it will).

Because, of course, missing out on an upside matters. How could it not? I should have sold the entirety of my position at the time, but I didn't because I lacked the conviction that the expected drop would come as clearly and as violently as it did.

In 2024, the company did well. The company, of course, is not immune to the global sort of challenges we're seeing, but it also demonstrated excellent resilience in a very problematic macro. The company's high-level savings program, BOOST, saw good results, and the company's already-excellent fundamental financial profile improved even further.

If you recall, the company is exposed to two markets for braking systems. Heavy vehicles and trains, to make it simple. The only thing that would make the company more attractive in my eyes is if the company was also exposed to the defense industry, but while some of the vehicles may be used that way, there's no clear sign of a clear defense end market to the company's revenue. If you look at my latest few articles in the German sector, you'll note that I picked up coverage and also looked at several industrial suppliers to the defense sector, including transmissions and components for tanks, naval vessels, and other military vehicles. It's part of my focus to cover this sector better in a world where the appeal seems very clear.

The company's RVS segment revenue share improved to above 50%, and the profit is improving. But the real thing I want to show you here is the company's efficiency progress - better shown in pictures than in words.

Brownfield and Greenfield is what the company does. Brown, for selling, Green for expanding/buying. The results of these have already shown in the boot line. 4 entities have been sold, with significant margin improvement at a relatively low cost in top line sales. All of the projects are on track, and the integration of the new entities is well underway. The company's total revenue is around €7.1B per year, and the company wants to keep close to €6B of this while fixing or selling €1.4B.

What will become abundantly clear to you in the valuation portion is that the time to buy Knorr-Bremse was under €55-€60/share, like me. We'll see if we even get back to that, but if we do, you can expect me to load up significant amounts of shares.

The company has won important contracts that are expected to add significant earnings.

There is a solid ongoing demand in the rail segment, with good growth not only in the EU but in NA as well. China is growing as well, and the fact that Knorr-Bremse is a European producer protects the company from tariff impacts. The Price/Cost gap that the company has had for some time has been closed as of 2024. The outlook for the coming year is good. Current numbers show us a book-to-bill of above 1x, high demand in the EU, okay demand in NA, and good demand in Asia. Trucks are more tricky for the company, as they always are, with weak demand across all geographic segments, but stability is expected for the coming next few months and quarters.

Nothing takes away from the company's very strong KPI's delivered, with an increase in FCF of over 20% and revenues very close to €8B for the year, with an order intake that exceeded €8.15B. The company's order book is well-filled at over €7B, and nothing can be argued against the company's fundamentals and balance sheet here - and I do mean almost nothing. The company's leverage is below 0.75x net debt /EBITDA, and both S&P and Moody's consider Knorr Bremse to be an A-rated/A3-rated company.

With these results, I don't see any issue with the company moving forward to higher results, and as a result of higher top-line, higher earnings justifying higher valuations.

Let's look at how this would look in terms of Knorr-Bremse valuation.

The valuation for Knorr-Bremse is positive with an upside, although not as much as before

Unfortunately, the upside isn't as much as we once had - but it's still decent enough.

Knorr-Bremse does rely quite a bit on a premiumization of its valuation. This premiumization is warranted, first of all. Why is it warranted? Because of solid fundamentals, including credit rating coupled with a yield that now is 2.3%, and set to grow to above 3% on the current price of €75/share native by 2027E, if these estimates are to be believed.

The first issue is that estimates and forecasts for this company are tricky because they "usually," by which I mean over 55% of the time with a 10-20% margin of error, fail in hitting as expected.

That does not change the fact that over the past 20 years, Knorr Bremse has averaged a 20-22x P/E, and that's also the P/E multiple that I intend to apply to the company going forward here. When I first started covering the company, even a conservative 17-20x p/E upside would bring about a 20%+ annualized RoR - but that is no longer the case.

If we assume the growth rate for he company is accurate here, a 3-year forecast at the company's 20-year average earnings multiple comes to an RoR of 65%, or 20% per year.

I consider this to be my base case. You could go higher, the company sometimes reaches as high as 22-24x P/E, which would raise this to 25% per year and over 75% RoR, my bullish case. Of course, it could also go down to 18x P/E, which would imply an annualized rate of return as low as 13%.

For a bearish case, I consider this to be entirely acceptable.

What I want to convey with regard to the valuation is that the company is in a position where even a bearish case now is sort of attractive, and I consider the base case very attractive. That means that my outlook, positive, has not changed since my last article, and has in fact strengthened.

I believe Knorr-Bremse deserves an upgrade in its price. My last PT was €95/share, and it was a downgrade I made because I believed the company to perform less than it did. Now I'm going to bump it again, to a 20x forward 2026E P/E, which comes to €103/share.

Peers? Some peers do exist, though I argue that no company does exactly what this one does - especially when you consider the attractive end markets, including rolling stock/trains. But if you go broader, to the machinery sector, you can find peers such as Cummins (CMI), Alstom (OTCPK:ALSMY), and maybe Allison Transmission (ALSN). But really, I wouldn't consider these more than peripherally comparable. Even if we look at them, Knorr is cheaper than ALSN, on the level of CMI and others. I would not say that Knorr-Bremse is either significantly more or significantly less attractive than its peers at this time.

So, from a historical perspective, the company has an upside, as well as from forecasts. From peers, I can't really see a valid comp situation, which leads me to work strictly from the forecasts and industry specifics.

I therefore say that Knorr Bremse is a "Buy" here with an attractive PT of €103/share and an upside at the very least realistically above 15% per year. That is what I am looking for, and for that reason, I say that we can "BUY" the company here.

The ADR for Knorr-Bremse is KNRRY, a 0.25x ADR, which means that 4 KNRRR equals one KBX share, the native. It means that my €103/share comes to around $29.5/share. I will say that we are now clearly noting the divergence between EUR/USD (EUR:USD) values as the USD is weakening in the wake of current trade conflicts. I therefore, even more than before, consider the native tickers to be the way to invest, not the ADR's, where possible.

Thesis

  • Knorr-Bremse is the world's leading manufacturer of braking systems for railways and with a strong presence in commercial trucking. It has absolutely stellar, A-rated fundamentals with a superb balance sheet and a very well-covered dividend. It's well-managed and, as of recent earnings, has a very well-filled order book.
  • At any cheap valuation below €70/share, this company becomes what I consider to be a cheap "Buy," even a strong one. The upside for the company is easily in double digits, without estimating too high or too crazy numbers.
  • At the current valuation, I consider Knorr to be a very solid "Buy" and I give the company an overall PT of €103/share, up from €95/share from my last article, based on what I believe to be a normalized fair-value target from growth rates and rocks-solid fundamentals. The 2024 results have only strengthened my resolve in this.
  • I believe Knorr is worth at least a forward 20-22x P/E with an EPS growth rate of 11.5% annualized for 2025E—this comes to around €103.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for a "Buy" rating. Based on this, and a valuation target of €95/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of November 2024.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Once Again Proves Its Safe Haven Mettle
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

Last week was a wild one for investors, with stocks whipsawing in a storm of volatility and uncertainty. When the dust settled, gold was the last safe haven standing.

Spot gold opened the week just below $3,040 an ounce. During Monday’s stock rout, the yellow metal plunged below $3,000 and tested $2,950.

That was as low as gold would go.

On Tuesday, gold stabilized and traded in a range between $2,980 and $3,017.

It’s not unusual for gold to fall sharply in the early stages of a stock market selloff. Because of its liquidity, traders often cash in gold to cover short positions in equities.

On Tuesday evening, gold started to rebound, climbing to $3,073 Wednesday morning before President Trump surprised everybody by announcing a 90-day reprieve on reciprocal tariffs.

Gold rebounded with other assets on the news. The rally ran out of steam in the stock market on Thursday, but gold kept climbing. By Friday, gold was at a new record, closing at $3,251.

Other safe-haven assets didn’t fare as well.

Bonds initially rallied early in the week, with yields on the 10-year Treasury dropping below 4 percent. However, bonds sold off later in the week even as stocks struggled after the brief Trump announcement rally. By Friday afternoon, the 10-year Treasury yield was at 4.49 percent. That was the highest level since February.

Rising yields signal a lack of demand for Treasuries. That's bad news for a government depending on borrowing to maintain its high-spending ways.

Meanwhile, the dollar index plunged. The ICE Dollar Index closed at 99.78, the lowest level since April 2022.

This was not business as usual during a time of stock market weakness and general market instability. As CNBC noted, “Treasuries and the dollar typically benefit from flight-to-safety environments, a function of the U.S.′ historical financial strength.”

That happened early in the trade war, but it quickly unraveled.

It's also very unusual for gold to rally when bond yields are rising. This underscores the fundamental strength of the yellow metal in the current market environment.

Bannockburn market strategist Marc Chandler called the bond selloff “severe” in an interview with Yahoo Finance, adding that we could be seeing a brewing “sell America” trade.

"People are concerned that maybe we're seeing a capital strike against the U.S., where large pools of capital are selling U.S. assets and taking their money home."

Deutsche Bank strategist George Saravelos also worried out loud about the trajectory of the dollar.

“The market is re-assessing the structural attractiveness of the dollar as the world’s global reserve currency and is undergoing a process of rapid de-dollarization. Nowhere is this more evident than the continued and combined collapse in the currency and U.S. bond market as this week comes to a close.”

Minneapolis Federal Reserve Bank President Neel Kashkari noted that a falling dollar in the midst of a trade war was not expected.

“Normally, when you see big tariff increases, I would have expected the dollar to go up. The fact that the dollar is going down at the same time I think lends some more credibility to the story of investor preferences changing.”

And what about gold?

“To the moon,” Chandler said.

“Many are talking about the capital strike against the U.S. and the demise of the greenback. Market turmoil and sharp drop in the dollar offsets the higher rates and drives the yellow metal to record levels. It is hard to talk about resistance, but the next target may be $3300, and $3500 in the slightly longer-term.”

Adrian Day Asset Management president Adrian Day agreed with the bullish sentiment, saying he thinks the trajectory of the yellow metal will continue upward.

“The latest pullback, like all the other recent pullbacks, was very short-lived. Gold clearly has momentum, with many buyers in the wings.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Aristotle Small/Mid Cap Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

The volatility observed throughout 2024, persisted into the first quarter of 2025. The Russell 2500 Index declined -7.50% during the quarter, following a strong 2024 with the index returning 11.99%. After a moderately positive start to the quarter, February and March were a more challenging market environment due to uncertainty surrounding the precise parameters and implementation of the new administration’s policies, geopolitical tensions and a higher for longer rate environment. Continued economic growth, a stable labor market and firmer inflation data kept the Federal Reserve (Fed) on the more hawkish course they took in December. Chairman Powell held rates steady at the March FOMC meeting and indicated the Fed will maintain a more measured approach going forward, updating their economic projections and forecasts to two rate cuts in 2025.

Stylistically, value stocks outperformed their growth counterparts during the quarter as the Russell 2500 Value Index returned -5.83% compared to the -10.80% return of the Russell 2500 Growth index. This is a reversal from last year where growth significantly outperformed value.

From a factor performance perspective, the quarter saw a change in market preference to higher-quality companies, favoring dividend-paying, defensive stocks. This change in sentiment was due in part to level interest rates, moderating US economic activity, and an increase in recession risk.

At the sector level, defensive sectors outperformed cyclical sectors, with Utilities (+8.37%), a sector often perceived as a bond proxy, being the only sector in the Russell 2500 Index to post a positive return during the quarter. The worst performing sectors were Information Technology (-16.57%), Consumer Discretionary (-11.77%), Industrials (-10.16%), and Health Care (-8.38%) while Utilities (+8.37%), Materials (-4.92%), Communications (-4.57%), Financials (-3.42%), Energy (-1.92%), Real Estate (-1.67%) and Consumer Staples (-1.02%) performed best.

Performance Review

For the first quarter, the Aristotle Small/Mid Cap Equity Composite generated a total return of -6.40% net of fees (-6.22% gross of fees), outperforming the -7.50% total return of the Russell 2500 Index. Outperformance was driven by security selection in the Health Care, Materials and Information Technology sectors coupled with an underweight allocation to Consumer Discretionary. An underweight to the Utilities sector, along with security selection in Consumer Staples, Consumer Discretionary and Energy detracted from performance.

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Alamos Gold (AGI) MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI)
Huron Consulting Group (HURN) Wolverine World Wide (WWW)
Merit Medical Systems (MMSI) Ciena Corporation (CIEN)
Chemed Corporation (CHE) Chart Industries (GTLS)
AerCap Holdings (AER) ASGN Incorporated (ASGN)

CONTRIBUTORS

Alamos Gold (AGI-CA), engages in the exploration, development, mining and extraction of precious metals. We maintain our investment as we believe in the company’s lower geopolitical risk profile, solid production growth plan from 600k oz to 1M oz per year over the next five years, and strong operational track record.

Huron Consulting Group (HURN), a specialty consulting company that provides financial, operational, and digital consulting services to health care, education and commercial clients, appreciated after delivering strong results highlighted by continued momentum within the company’s health care and commercial segments, more than offsetting the education segment. We maintain our investment, as we believe the company remains well-positioned to capitalize on a demand backdrop aided by financial and operational pressures in its largest end-markets, along with secular tailwinds supporting digital transformation, analytics and cloud consulting quarter of 2025.

DETRACTORS

MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI), a designer and manufacturer of high-performance semiconductor products, declined along with the broader semiconductor industry during the quarter. We maintain our position, as we believe the company’s meaningful exposure to growing demand from Data Center and 5G end market applications along with the integration of recent acquisitions should drive additional shareholder value in periods to come.

Wolverine World Wide (WWW), engages in the design, manufacture, and sale of branded casual, active lifestyle, work, outdoor sport, athletic, uniform, footwear, and apparel. The company met expectations but guided lower for FY 2025, punishing the stock price. We maintain our position as we expect improved top-line growth and further net leverage reduction to support the Company’s long-term financial objectives leading to increased shareholder value.

Recent Portfolio Activity

Buys/Acquisitions Sells/Liquidations
Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) Barnes Group (B)
Old National Bancorp (ONB) Designer Brands (DBI)
Valvoline (VVV) Nasdaq (NDAQ)
Summit Materials (SUM)

BUYS/ACQUISITIONS

Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), is a real estate investment trust that owns, manages and develops primarily neighborhood community shopping centers and single tenant properties leased to major retail tenants such as Sherwin-Williams, Wal-Mart and TJX Companies. We believe the company’s high-quality portfolio, recession resistant retail categories and strong balance sheet coupled with management’s proactive tenant management and acquisition pipeline will benefit the company on a go-forward basis.

Old National Bancorp (ONB), is a regional bank serving clients primarily in the Midwest and Southeastern U.S. We believe the company’s geographic location, strong balance sheet, increased loan growth and repricing of fixed-rate loans to higher rates will benefit NIMs on a go-forward basis.

Valvoline (VVV), is a pure-play auto quick lube and maintenance service provider with over 2,000 locations in the U.S. and Canada. The company provides quick and convenient stay-in-your-car automotive preventative maintenance through its full-service oil changes from skilled technicians in less than 15minutes. We believe the company will benefit from increased market share gains, driven by both corporate and franchise expansion, growing its scale, accelerating growth and increasing its moat.

SELLS/LIQUIDATIONS

Barnes Group (B), is a globally recognized industrial and aerospace manufacturer and service provider that stands out for its highly engineered products, differentiated industrial technologies, and innovative solutions. The company was acquired and taken private by Apollo Funds.

Designer Brands (DBI), is a North American company which engages in the design, production, and retail of footwear and accessory brands. We sold the position due to what we believed were deteriorating corporate fundamentals.

Nasdaq (NDAQ), is a holding company, which engages in trading, clearing, exchange technology, regulatory, securities listing, information, and public and private company services. We sold the position as the stock had reached our valuation target.

Summit Materials (SUM), is a North American supplier of aggregates, cement, and ready-mix concrete for the construction industry. The company was acquired by Quikrete Holdings, Inc.

Outlook

We remain optimistic about the long-term potential for the SMID-cap segment of the U.S. market. Valuations remain compelling relative to large caps, with the Russell 2500 Index trading near the lower end of its historical range. Potential tailwinds, including deregulation, lower corporate tax rates, increased M&A activity, continued reshoring of U.S. manufacturing, and infrastructure-related spending, could provide additional support for SMID-cap stocks. There may be some short-term volatility as the new administration’s policies are implemented and absorbed by markets. We also remain mindful of risks such as inflation reaccelerating, increased geopolitical tensions, and potential U.S. economic weakness.

Positioning

Our current positioning is a function of our bottom-up security selection process and our ability to identify what we view as attractive investment candidates, regardless of economic sector definitions. Overweights in Information Technology and Industrials are mostly a function of our underlying company specific views rather than any top-down predictions for each sector. Conversely, we continue to be underweight in Consumer Discretionary, as we have been unable to identify what we consider to be compelling long-term opportunities that fit our discipline given the rising risk profiles of many retail businesses and a potential deceleration in goods spending following a period of strength. We also underweight in Financials as those are the companies in that sector who meet our rigorous requirements on a fundamental basis. Given our focus on long-term business fundamentals, patient investment approach and low portfolio turnover, the strategy’s sector positioning generally does not change significantly from quarter to quarter. However, we may take advantage of periods of volatility by adding selectively to certain companies when appropriate.

Disclosures

The opinions expressed herein are those of Aristotle Capital Boston, LLC (Aristotle Boston) and are subject to change without notice.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. The information provided in this report should not be considered financial advice or a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no assurance that any securities discussed herein will remain in an account’s portfolio at the time you receive this report or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities discussed may not represent an account’s entire portfolio and, in the aggregate, may represent only a small percentage of an account’s portfolio holdings. The performance attribution presented is of a representative account from Aristotle Boston’s Small/Mid Cap Equity Composite. The representative account is a discretionary client account which was chosen to most closely reflect the investment style of the strategy. The criteria used for representative account selection is based on the account’s period of time under management and its similarity of holdings in relation to the strategy. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions, holdings or sectors discussed were or will be profitable, or that the investment recommendations or decisions Aristotle Boston makes in the future will be profitable or equal the performance of the securities discussed herein. Aristotle Boston reserves the right to modify its current investment strategies and techniques based on changing market dynamics or client needs. Recommendations made in the last 12 months are available upon request.

Returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses.

As of December 31, 2014, there were no non-fee-paying accounts in the Composite.

All investments carry a certain degree of risk, including the possible loss of principal. Investments are also subject to political, market, currency and regulatory risks or economic developments. International investments involve special risks that may in particular cause a loss in principal, including currency fluctuation, lower liquidity, different accounting methods and economic and political systems, and higher transaction costs.

These risks typically are greater in emerging markets. Securities of small‐ and medium‐sized companies tend to have a shorter history of operations, be more volatile and less liquid. Value stocks can perform differently from the market as a whole and other types of stocks.

The material is provided for informational and/or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as investment, legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Investors should consult their financial and tax adviser before making investments.

The opinions referenced are as of the date of publication, may be modified due to changes in the market or economic conditions, and may not necessarily come to pass.

Aristotle Capital Boston, LLC is an independent investment adviser registered under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, as amended. Registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. More information about Aristotle Boston, including our investment strategies, fees and objectives, can be found in our Form ADV Part 2, which is available upon request. ACB-2504-20

Performance Disclosures

Sources: CAPS Composite Hub, Russell Investments

Composite returns for periods ended March 31, 2025, are preliminary pending final account reconciliation.

*The Aristotle Small/Mid Cap Equity Composite has an inception date of January 1, 2008, at a predecessor firm. During this time, Jack McPherson and Dave Adams had primary responsibility for managing the strategy. Performance starting January 1, 2015, was achieved at Aristotle Boston.

As of December 31, 2014, there were no non-fee-paying accounts in the Composite. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Performance results for periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses. Please see important disclosures enclosed within this document.

Index Disclosures

The Russell 2500 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2500 Growth® Index measures the performance of the small/mid cap companies located in the United States that also exhibit a growth probability. The Russell 2500 Value® Index measures the performance of the small/mid cap companies located in the United States that also exhibit a value probability. The volatility (beta) of the composite may be greater or less than the benchmarks. It is not possible to invest directly in these indices.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Peer Comparison For Walmart: Valuation Keeps It A Hold
data.description:
Rush to stock up on groceries has hit Walmart (NYSE:WMT) as pandemic like fears of empty or costlier Walmart shelves is doing the rounds. But should investors flock to WMT shares, particularly as an opportunity presents itself after the recent corrections? Several investors and analysts like WMT now as it is believed to be recession proof. However, 2008 and 2020 were more demand side shocks. This downturn, as it unfolds, may have more to do with supply shocks and Walmart’s behavior could be different. We look at how WMT is placed in relation to its peers Amazon (AMZN) and Target (TGT) to assess whether it is good to invest now.

Market Types and How Tariff Will impact

WMT is heavily skewed towards a brick and mortar structure. However, WMT is expanding visibly into online segments, what WMT calls an omnichannel business structure. Ecommerce fulfillment, also comes with its own infrastructure of warehouses and logistics, but remains less capex heavy than location sensitive final mile delivery points.

For Amazon, it is the reverse, with physical store experience being brought in much later and still contributing smaller footprints to overall revenue. Walmart is more comparable to Target in how it is setup with focus on physical retail. Walmart’s grocery focus is more in line with a physical presence strategy, compared to Amazon’s more diverse product offering. This has helped Walmart clock robust performance over the years. However, I still believe, the growth potential of the ecommerce biased model is higher due to lower investments, easier expansion into new markets and benefits of scale. Amazon, just comparing the retail arm, should not only benefit from this growth model, but also leverage its wide array of focus areas in terms of product offerings to the customer.

Target, with focus on discretionary spending, is likely to suffer more in the tariff era (waiting to see how that pans out), plus any economic downturn will likely hit discretionary spending first before groceries. All the three retailers are taking measures to negotiate and rework the supply chain. The impact of tariffs will definitely hit all three, but the extent is to be seen, as the trade agreements story unfolds. Walmart seems to be placed ahead of Target in terms of being in a more resilient market but behind Amazon.

Very Sound Financials Reported by All Three

Walmart is slightly ahead of even Amazon in terms of revenue, but at lower gross profit margins. The debt to equity ratio is excellent for Walmart, very very healthy for a physical retail business – Target suffers here. Amazon leads debt utilization efficiencies due to its diverse business lines. In RoE terms, Target is placed very well. There is no case against Walmart simply looking at financials. In free cash flow terms, Amazon’s higher revenue base and scale of operations help a significantly healthier number. Target has been showing the highest growth in free cash flow which is a positive. Overall, Walmart holds up relatively well on all parameters.

How Does the Future Look for Walmart

The path ahead for Walmart remains very exciting with digital transformations and ecommerce focus clearly marked out. Walmart is bringing in AI based personalization and other user experience changes such as social commerce and seamless payments. Digital advertising is also another area that will likely grow in the coming years. Walmart has been ahead of the curve in terms of customer engagement, as backed by its sound financial performance seen earlier.

Walmart has been quietly expanding globally as well – notably Sam’s Club in China and Flipkart partnership in India. Repercussions of the trade war with China could not only have impacts from a tariff perspective, but the brand damage of WMT units in China is also not ruled out. India is a relatively stable bet, with Flipkart enjoying a marketshare as large (slightly larger in fact) as Amazon’s. Walmart is also ramping up presence in South and Central America. The revenue generated from international operations is already ~20%, similar range as Amazon.

Valuation and Corrections

All the three retail giants show definite strength in their own core areas. Target’s financial performance stays robust in a lot of parameters. Tariff impact could be the worst here though. Walmart is less likely to be tariff struck because of the grocery segment emphasis – relatively speaking. Financials are extremely sound for Walmart too. Amazon is a different beast and I have explored its strong case in a separate article, Amazon is a decent buy for its multiple business lines, several of which are tariff proof as well. Financial Performance also is unquestionably strong.

With little to choose so far, it all boils down to valuations. There is merit in buying either of the three provided the valuations are comfortable. Target is trading at a forward P/E of 9.75. It has also corrected the most, by a margin ~35% in the past 3 months, including the recent selloff. Amazon has also corrected by about ~22% in the 3 month window and has a forward P/E now of 26.93. The Amazon comparison is not an apple to apple comparison given its value unlocking capability from different and new business lines, even in the near future. Walmart is valued at a forward P/E of 31 and has not corrected significantly yet. The 3 month drop is at ~15% levels.

Hold Walmart

What works for Walmart is its strong financials, debt to equity ratio, great free cash flow history and a sound RoE for a physical business. The tariff story is also supportive for WMT’s case when compared to TGT. The biggest strength is WMT’s excellent historical performance despite concerns around its brick and mortar model.

However, while demanding an Amazon like valuation, value unlocking in retail alone is a far less attractive story than Amazon’s strong tech and futuristic AI potential. Walmart remains a robust performer, and matches Amazon in revenue terms today, despite the capex heavy physical bias. However, I see no great lever that can unlock value for WMT when compared to AMZN. Add to that the fact that WMT has not corrected as much as its peers. Despite being a strong stock, I will shy away from a “Buy” call. Amazon remains a “Buy”. Target can also be considered if an entry into the retail space is intended.

My call for Walmart is “Hold” till there is further correction of at least 10% or time corrections and investors can enter at more favorable entry points. Existing investors can continue to hold WMT though – WMT remains an intrinsically strong stock to have in the portfolio.

data.title: The Money Supply Keeps Growing As The Fed Backs Off Monetary 'Tightening'
data.description:
Money-supply growth rose year over year in February for the seventh month in a row, the first time this has happened since mid-2022. The current trend in money-supply growth suggests a continued reversal of more than a year of historically large contractions in the money supply that occurred throughout much of 2023 and 2024. As of February, the money supply appears to be continuing in a period of moderate monetary growth coming out of a period of historically large swings in monetary trends from early 2020 to mid-2024.

In February, year-over-year growth in the money supply was at 2.75 percent. That’s slightly below December’s 28-month high of 2.79, February reported the second-largest year-over-year increase since September 2022. February’s growth rate was up from January’s rate of 2.34 percent. It’s also a big change from February 2024’s year-over-year decline of 5.62 percent. Last year, the US money supply was still in the midst of the largest drop in money supply we’ve seen since the Great Depression. Prior to 2023, at no other point for at least sixty years had the money supply fallen by so much.

Following the big swings experienced during 2023 and 2024, money supply growth over the past six months has settled into a relatively moderate growth rate of one to three percent. During that time, the total increase in the money supply has been 1.7 percent, and the money supply slightly declined from December 2024 to February 2025. Looking at month-to-month changes in the money supply, we also find a moderate upward trend. Money supply fell 0.61 percent from December to January, and then moved slightly upward (+0.13) from January to February. Month-to-month growth in the money supply had accelerated through most of the second half of 2024 but then moderated as the year ended and flattened in early 2025:

The money supply metric used here—the “true,” or Rothbard-Salerno, money supply measure (TMS)—is the metric developed by Murray Rothbard and Joseph Salerno, and is designed to provide a better measure of money supply fluctuations than M2.

In recent months, M2 growth rates have followed a similar course to TMS growth rates, although M2 is growing faster than TMS. In February, the M2 growth rate was 3.90 percent. That’s up from January’s growth rate of 3.89 percent. February’s growth rate was also up from February 2024’s rate of negative 1.44 percent. Month over month, however, M2 decreased by -0.12 percent from January to February. That’s the largest month-to-month decline in ten months. Nonetheless, the overall trend for both TMS and M2 continues upward.

Although year-over-year and month-to-month growth rates may be moderating, money-supply totals remain far above what they were before 2020 and the covid panic. From 2020 to 2022, the Federal Reserve’s easy-money policies resulted in approximately 6.4 trillion dollars being added to the economy. This was done to help to finance the federal government’s enormous deficits driven by runaway covid stimulus programs.

Since 2009, the TMS money supply is now up by more than 192 percent. (M2 has grown by 152 percent in that period.) Out of the current money supply of $19.4 trillion, nearly 26 percent of that has been created since January 2020. Since 2009, in the wake of the global financial crisis, more than $12 trillion of the current money supply has been created. In other words, nearly two-thirds of the total existing money supply have been created just in the past thirteen years.

As a result money-supply is now well above the former trend that was in place before 2020. For example, just to get to back to the money-creation trend that existed in 2019 before the “great covid inflation,” total money supply would need to fall by at least three trillion dollars.

In spite of this, the Federal Reserve has shown no appetite for “unwinding” the enormous amounts of new money created since the 2020 covid panic, and the Fed continues to proceed at a snail’s pace when it comes to reducing the Fed’s balance sheet back down to the more “normal” levels that prevailed before the Fed embarked on “quantitative easing” in 2009.

This became clear in September of last year when, in spite of the fact that price-inflation levels had not returned to the Fed’s much-touted two-perfect price-inflation target, the Fed began cutting its target interest rate in an effort to force down interest rates. Over the next three months, the Fed reduced its target policy rate by 100 basis points. This premature turn back to dovish policy paced the way for December 28-month high in monetary growth.

Shortly thereafter, the Fed abandoned its previously stated goals for shrinking its balance sheet. At its March meeting, the Fed’s FOMC, which had previously pledged to reduce its treasury holdings by as much as $25 billion per month, reduced the cap to only $5 billion per month. The FOMC has also capped its mortgage asset monthly drawdown to only $35 billion.

In other words, the March reversal was just the latest capitulation in what the Fed has insisted is an ongoing policy of “quantitative tightening.”

Why do these drawdowns matter? During the global financial crisis and during the covid panic, the Federal Reserve bought up trillions of dollars in mortgage debts and US treasuries to prop up the mortgage industry and to make it easier for the federal government to finance its enormous deficits. These huge purchases put trillions of dollars of new money into the economy. In contrast, when these asset holdings are reduced, that removes money from the economy, and the dollar would regain some of the purchasing power destroyed by monetary inflation. Although the Fed has long claimed that it is committed to reducing these asset holdings—which function as inflationary subsidies for the federal government and for the mortgage industry—the Fed’s total asset holdings still remain well above where they were as recently as 2019.

The creation of this huge portfolio of Federal Reserve assets has created a second major tool for the Fed in its open market operations. As a result, if the Fed is serious about partially undoing the damage it has done with its inflationary monetary policy, the Fed needs to both allow interest rates to rise and aggressively reduce its assets.

It is clear the Fed has little interest in doing either.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Fog Of Tariffs
data.description:
There was a near-record number of job layoff announcements during the month of March, according to the labor research firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas. There was a total of 275,240 layoff announcements last month. The level of layoffs was eclipsed once before in history when the nation was shutting down during the COVID pandemic outbreak in March of 2020.

The ISM Services Index for March slowed to 50.8, down from 53.5 in February. The Employment Index dropped into contraction territory for the first time in six months; the reading of 46.2 percent is a 7.7-percentage point decrease, compared to the 53.9 recorded in February. Meanwhile, the NFIB Small Business Optimism index for March fell by the most month-over-month since June 2022.

In sharp contrast, the non-farm payroll report for March showed an increase of 228,000 net new jobs created. But this was up from the revised number of 117,000 in February, which was much lower than the 151k number found in the original February report. The estimate for March was for just 140,000 jobs created. However, the unemployment rate did tick up to 4.2% from 4.1%. This somewhat rosy data from the Labor Department contradicts most of the other jobs data from private sources.

However, this does put a Gordian knot in Fed Chair Powell’s hands. The hard economic data is still strong, and the inflation data is too high for pre-emptive rate cuts to occur. Even though tariffs virtually guarantee that a recession will happen sooner rather than later, they also push prices significantly higher. Inflation is projected to rise even faster than the current 3% pace. It is already at a level that has decimated the middle class. In fact, the middle class is on life support, and the divide between the rich and the poor has never been greater. For the first time in history, the top 1% of earners have more wealth than the entire middle class.

But now, falling asset prices are destroying even the balance sheet of those who inhabit the top quintile. The S&P 500 lost 10% of its value last Thursday and Friday alone, following President Trump’s "Liberation Day" tariff announcement made on Wednesday evening.

The reason for the plunge is clear: Tariffs were - at least at first promulgated - much larger than expected. What was supposed to be just reciprocal duties placed on some nations became across-the-board taxes that were much higher. For example, the tariff on China has now been raised to 145%, although Trump caved in due to pressure from a cratering stock and bond market and paused most duties for 90 days.

But are stocks cheap now after this retreat? The S&P 500 is trading at 20x next year’s 7 percent projected earnings growth. That is a rich multiple on an earnings growth rate that will most likely never happen. What is much more likely to occur is a lower multiple on earnings that are shrinking.

President Trump has stated that he wants to rebuild America’s manufacturing base, balance our trade deficit, and use tariff revenue to eliminate our fiscal deficit and pay for tax cuts. If that is indeed the case, there really cannot be any trade deals of significance. Zeroing out tariffs will not accomplish any of the President’s goals. For example, Vietnam factory workers make about $3k per year on average. Chinese workers make $8k. Whereas US factory workers make $35k. Therefore, even if all the existing tariffs are eliminated, it will do little to balance our trade deficit. Nor will it reduce our fiscal deficit one iota. It also cannot lead to a manufacturing renaissance. And there will be no money raised to offset tax cuts. However, if the view of this administration is just to lower duties on the margin, they will in no way be large enough to offset the wage differentials, government support measures, and regulatory advantages found outside of the US - in other words, those non-tariff barriers used to gain an advantage over America.

Tariffs are taxes. And we still have a 10% rate for every nation and those onerous 145% rates for China. When you combine that tax hike with a falling labor force, the end of government stimulus, reduced liquidity, and a reverse wealth effect, you get an expedited and intensifying economic contraction. The US economy was on a slow glide path down towards a recession and a liquidity crisis before this trade war began. Regardless of Trump’s tariff twists, our Inflation/Deflation and Economic Cycle model is still indicating that a recession is coming. The real danger is the recession will be a deep one.

Most advisors say to just hang in there because the market always comes back. You will never hear that buy and hold B.S. at Pento Portfolio Strategies. This time around, neither the Fed or Treasury have the cover to act until the carnage is absolute. In other words, the government “put” will arrive even later than usual, which, according to recent history, has arrived too late to halt the damage until half of your money has evaporated. Active and smart management has never been more critical.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls Stock: The Bear Case Is Showing Cracks, Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
President Trump's second term has already been quite an interesting one for stock investors with policy changes and tariff announcements leading to wild swings in stock prices. In the last three trading sessions, industrial giant Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (NYSE:HII) stock has risen almost 20% after the President signed an executive order outlining new guidelines and initiatives to boost domestic maritime capabilities. Last Friday, HII stock got another boost when Goldman Sachs analysts double-upgraded the stock from sell to buy after digesting the expected positive impact of the Trump administration's support to the shipbuilding industry. HII stock gained more than 7% following this upgrade. For long-term HII investors who have weathered many storms since Covid, these recent developments come as music to their ears.

The short-term market reaction to favorable policy developments is understandable. However, the important question is whether this momentum will continue in the foreseeable future. Upon analyzing the operating margin pressures faced by the company, recent policy changes, the backlog, and the U.S. Navy's ambitious plans, I believe Huntington Ingalls is at an inflection point. Although impressive short-term returns are unlikely, I am bullish on the prospects for HII for the next five years.

The Policy Catalyst Marks A Potential Inflection Point For HII

The executive order signed by President Trump last Wednesday aims to "restore America's maritime dominance". As part of this policy, a Maritime Action Plan will be formed to provide actionable strategies to strengthen the maritime capabilities of the nation. This order comes at a time when the U.S. is going into a trade war with China. The latter has dominated the shipbuilding industry in recent times. Below are some facts highlighted by the executive order last Wednesday.

  • 0.2% of the world’s ships are built by the United States, compared to 74% built by China.
  • 0% of the containers used to move commodities around the world are built by the United States, compared to 96% built by China.
  • 0% of the ship-to-shore cranes in the United States are built domestically, compared to 80% built by China.

The executive order highlights the need for swift action to restore America's dominance in this industry, which suggests domestic shipbuilders are likely to benefit from a strong policy tailwind in the foreseeable future, potentially leading to accelerated approval of contracts, lower taxes, and government funding support/incentives to promote shipyard investments.

For Huntington Ingalls, the largest military seapower provider in the U.S., this favorable policy change should result in quantifiable benefits in the form of a growing backlog and higher revenues. Also, this executive order mitigates some negative impacts arising from planned defense budget cuts as we can expect a higher proportion of the defense budget to be deployed to support the shipbuilding industry. For context, the Pentagon has announced an 8% budget cut, totaling a $50 billion reduction in expenditure annually for the next five years.

In addition, the executive order aims to streamline the procurement process in the shipbuilding industry and also address problems in workforce development. These are some key challenges Huntington Ingalls has faced in the recent past.

The bull case for HII is easy to understand. Huntington Ingalls builds mission-critical assets used by the military and is the leader in the shipbuilding space, the demand for these assets is likely to grow during President Trump's second term, and some recent challenges faced by the company will be addressed by the new executive order. However, this is just one side of the story. To determine HII's appeal as a long-term investment, investors should evaluate whether the policy catalyst is sufficient to fully address the margin pressures faced by the company.

The Bear Case Is Centered on Margin Pressures

Policy support is critical for a company that builds ships for the military. However, policy support alone does not guarantee improved profitability. On one hand, it will probably take much longer than the market anticipates for the executive order to offer tangible benefits to Huntington Ingalls as setting up contracts takes time. A policy implementation lag has to be expected here and investors should also keep a close eye on overall defense budget growth (or the lack of it) as the negative impact of budget cuts may offset the favorable impact from policy tailwinds. Although these risks need to be monitored, I am more concerned about the recent margin pressures faced by HII. As long as an improvement in margins is on the cards, I would feel comfortable taking a position in HII as a strategic investment to hedge the impact of higher tariffs.

To set the stage, let's establish that Huntington Ingalls has faced major margin problems since Covid.

HII operating margin

There are a few reasons behind this notable decline in operating margins.

Cost inflation has been a major factor contributing to this erosion in margins. As is standard practice, Huntington Ingalls generally enters into fixed-price contracts with its customers. The contracts that were signed before COVID-19 proved to be a drag on profitability in the last three years as surging inflation led to much higher costs than projected by the company, impacting profit margins.

Labor market challenges have amplified costs in recent times too. Skilled labor shortages have forced Huntington Ingalls to pay top dollar to attract suitable talent, boosting operating expenses. This is evident in the substantial decline in operating margins at the Newport News Shipyard (from around 8% to just 4%) due to significant labor shortages.

Without a proper plan to improve margins meaningfully, HII is unlikely to see a major expansion in valuation multiples. The company is currently valued at a forward P/E of 16. This decline in margins affected free cash flows last year too, with FCF declining to just $40 million despite projecting FCF exceeding $600 million. In 2023, the company recorded an FCF of $692 million.

For the current financial year, Huntington Ingalls expects FCF of at least $300 million on the back of a shipbuilding operating margin of around 6%.

FY 2025 outlook

The company's focus on improving operating margins is encouraging. So far, Huntington Ingalls has announced several measures to boost operating efficiency.

Some measures taken include actively pursuing shipbuilding outsourcing measures to manage the workload more efficiently and in a timely manner. As confirmed during the earnings call last February, the goal is to outsource approximately 30% of shipbuilding workloads this year.

In an even more encouraging development, the company has entered into renegotiations with the U.S. Navy to align contract prices to reflect the recent surge in costs. If successful, contract renegotiations will prove to be a catalyst that drives operating margins higher, potentially improving the investor sentiment toward HII.

With a longer-term perspective, Huntington Ingalls is investing in predictive analytics, AI, and the modernization of production facilities as well. However, these technological investments are unlikely to yield material returns as early as this financial year.

The bear case for HII, which is centered on the company's margin pressures, is not as relevant as it was a couple of years ago. Although progress will be gradual, I believe Huntington Ingalls' margins have reached a trough - or at least close to a trough. The bear case, therefore, is showing some cracks.

The Big Picture Is Decent

Huntington Ingalls is not a high-tech company that is poised to disrupt a fast-growing business sector. For a company of its scale and size, the industry outlook is decent despite the threat posed by defense budget cuts.

As part of the newly released long-term shipbuilding plan, the U.S. Navy aims to increase the number of battle force ships it owns from 295 today to 390 by 2054. Overall, the Navy is expected to purchase a total of 364 new combat ships and support ships. As per the analysis of the Congressional Budget Office, the Navy's ambitious plans would require shipyards to produce "substantially more naval tonnage than they have produced over the last 10 years".

It is also worth noting that the Navy plans to acquire 134 unmanned ships and submarines to complement its manned fleet as well.

To meet these goals, the U.S. Navy would be forced to boost its shipbuilding budget in the coming years, creating new contracting opportunities for Huntington Ingalls. The company ended 2024 with a total backlog of $48.7 billion, which is a noteworthy amount given that it recorded revenue of $11.5 billion for 2024. According to Fitch Ratings, this substantial backlog offers impressive revenue visibility through 2028.

Although the FCF position was disappointing last year, with almost $2.5 billion in liquidity, Huntington Ingalls does not face a credit crunch today. Starting in 2026, I believe the operating margin profile will improve, paving the way for higher FCF.

Takeaway

I am turning bullish on HII stock as I believe the company is nearing a trough for operating margins. The industry outlook is decent, and the company will benefit from favorable policy decisions in the coming years as the Trump administration sets a path for the American shipbuilding industry to gain some ground over China. The biggest catalyst for an expansion in valuation multiples will be a notable improvement in margins. Although this is unlikely to happen in the first half of the year, I expect meaningful improvements in the latter half. Strategically gaining some exposure to HII will help investors hedge against the impact of tariffs as well, which sweetens the deal for HII. Key risks to monitor include the impact of defense budget cuts and prolonged delays in enacting the measures outlined in last week's executive order.

data.title: Toyota May Benefit From A Tighter Market
data.description:
Toyota Motor Corporation (NYSE:TM) shares have performed exceptionally well when compared to peer automotive OEMs following Liberation Day, with shares down a mere -2.55%. With a fleet of value-oriented vehicles, Toyota may be well-positioned to take on additional market share, particularly as a result of tighter consumer discretionary spending as a result of elevated inflation, interest rates, and the potential additional costs associated with tariffs. With the positive outlook for Toyota, I am recommending TM shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $185/share at 8.63x eFY26 EV/EBITDA.

Toyota Motor Corporation Operations

Toyota is focusing on developing its personnel while investing in growth opportunities to improve operations, investing ¥832b. This includes the development and production of battery-electric vehicles for its Lexus models, particularly for the Chinese market. Management is planning to begin production of Lexus BEVs in Shanghai, China, in 2027 at 100,000 annual units.

Toyota also expanded its U.S.-based battery production for hybrid and battery-electric vehicles, which was expected to begin production in April 2025. This $14b battery plant is located in North Carolina and is expected to produce 30GWh annually.

This can potentially be an important move for Toyota, especially when considering the possibility of tariffs on trade between the U.S. and Japan. The current tariff situation is relatively uncertain as the U.S. imposed a 25% tariff on automotive imports on Japan before the unilateral 24% tariff on Japanese imports. Following the broader imposition, the Trump Administration reduced the 24% rate down to 10% for 90 days during trade negotiations. Despite this policy change, the 25% tariff on the automotive industry remained in place.

The existing tariffs can potentially impact sales in North America as Toyota has a manufacturing deficit of roughly 764 units in the last four quarters. This means that Toyota sold 764 more units than it produced in North America.

This could potentially either result in slower sales in North America or a lower operating margin for the market segment. YTD, the operating margin for North American sales are already tight at 1.17%, far below the 14.25% realized in the Japanese market.

Accordingly, Japanese leaders are seeking a resolution rather than a trade war with the U.S., potentially leading to either reduced import duties, an improved trade balance, or maintaining the current policy in place. This may mean that by the end of the 90-day grace period, the import tax on Japanese vehicles may either be reduced or eliminated.

Though the 25% tariff on Japan will directly impact Toyota, the U.S. OEMs may also be impacted by a 25% trade tariff on imports from Canada & Mexico, including automotive, steel, and aluminum. This may mean that Toyota will not be the only automotive OEM facing higher costs and pricing pressure for vehicle sales.

If this is the case, Toyota could potentially be in a position to take on additional market share as the OEM’s vehicles are seen as the best value for the price. Morningstar sees U.S. auto sales growing to 16.2-16.4mm units for 2025, a 2.8% increase when compared to the previous year. As of 2024, Toyota held 15% of the U.S. market share, only second to General Motors (GM). This could be amplified given the potential for a 1mm unit decline in US auto sales as a result of the international tariff imposition.

Toyota Motor Corporation Financial Position

Toyota reduced its automotive production by -5% on a global basis in q3 ’25 with Europe being most heavily impacted. North American production declined by -1.7% in the quarter. This was likely due to the OEM attempting to match production to unit sales, which experienced a -4% global decline. North American sales declined by -10% in q3 ’25.

I suspect the decline in unit sales was the result of a more challenging consumer market rather than tariffs. Consumer discretionary spending has become significantly more selective, leading to consumers trading down in order to maintain a similar purchase volume. As a result of continued inflationary pressure on essential items like food and shelter, I suspect that discretionary spending may remain challenged throughout 2025, potentially leading to consumers either purchasing less vehicles or trading down to more budget-friendly models. This may either position Toyota well for the current environment or put Toyota in the same position as the more premium automotive OEMs.

For Q4 ’25, I’m forecasting Toyota to generate $77b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $0.31/share. This would produce $309b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $2.29/share for eFY25.

Risks Related to Toyota Motor Corporation

Bull Case

Tariffs and inflation may benefit Toyota to a certain degree as the automotive OEM is seen as a value-oriented vehicle maker, potentially capturing consumers seeking a more budget-friendly model. Toyota also has a global manufacturing presence, allowing for the firm to effectively navigate through the current trade regime, potentially minimizing the impact to costs.

Bear Case

Consumer discretionary spending remains tight, potentially leading to a more challenging vehicle sales market for CY25. With interest rates remaining relatively elevated, consumers may put off purchasing a new vehicle unless absolutely necessary.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

TM shares currently trade at 8.78x EV/EBITDA on a trailing twelve-month basis. TM trades at a slight premium to its peer average of 9.50x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis.

TM shares have performed exceptionally well since Liberation Day on April 2, 2025. This may suggest that the market sees Toyota having a minimal impact to tariffs, a trade deal will be enacted to benefit the firm, or that the firm may have a durable advantage over competing OEMs as a value-oriented vehicle maker.

Given the potentially positive outlook for Toyota, I believe TM shares should be priced at $185/share at 8.63x eFY26 EV/EBITDA. In addition to the positive outlook for share price performance, TM shares pay out a robust dividend rate of $5.46/share, yielding 3.22% at the current price level. I am recommending TM shares with a BUY rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FCPI: This Inflation ETF Isn't As One-Dimensional As You Might Think
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When you name a fund the Fidelity Stocks for Inflation ETF (BATS:FCPI), you pretty much box yourself into a corner, at least from the perspective of most retail investors. With inflation registering at 2.4% last month compared to a 9.1% peak in June 2022, there doesn't seem to be the need for an ETF like FCPI. Indeed, FCPI's fund flows declined by $60.9 million over the last three years.

Yet, FCPI continues to deliver decent returns. I last covered it on March 3, 2023, when I rated it a "buy" after considering its substantial turnover. Since that article was published, FCPI has gained 33.06% compared to 34.89% for iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). It also trounced the ProShares Equities for Rising Rates ETF (EQRR), another potential option, given how inflation and interest rates often move hand in hand. In my view, that's pretty good for a fund that supposedly only does well in high-inflation environments.

The purpose of this article is to evaluate FCPI fundamentally and see if it's still a good pick, especially given how many of President Trump's policies promote inflation. As I'll demonstrate, FCPI's Index has robust quality, value, and momentum screens and only tilts towards inflation-friendly sectors. This makes it well-balanced on many factors, and its quality and value features are particularly attractive regardless of whether inflation picks up again or not. Therefore, I'm comfortable with FCPI as a long-term investment, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

FCPI Overview

Strategy Discussion

FCPI tracks the Fidelity Stocks For Inflation Factor Index, which launched on September 3, 2019, two months before the ETF launched. Its methodology is linked here, and I've summarized the key points below.

1. The Index universe is the largest 1,000 U.S. stocks based on market cap after applying certain liquidity and investability screens, including those with less than six months' price history and those with less than 15% free float market cap. The Russell 1000 Index is a good proxy.

2. For each security, the Index Provider calculates a composite factor score based on three factors weighted equally: value, quality, and momentum.

3. For non-bank stocks, example factors include free cash flow yield, return on invested capital, and volatility-adjusted price returns, with weights as follows:

4. For bank stocks, the value and quality factors differ and include a security's tangible book value to price and return on equity, with weights as follows:

5. The Index adds a size factor adjustment that assigns greater weight to smaller companies.

6. The Index selects roughly 100 stocks, and the number of stocks in each sector is based on the aggregate weight of each sector. Using the universe's sector allocations as a baseline, the Index adjusts +/- 5% to sectors/industries thought to perform strongly/poorly in rising inflation environments, as follows:

7. The Index reconstitutes on the third Friday in February and August.

Sector Allocations and Performance Snapshot

One reason why FCPI has performed well even as inflation has retreated is that it only applies reasonably small sector tilts in favor of sectors like Energy and Materials and at the expense of others like Consumer Discretionary and Technology. To illustrate, consider how FCPI's current sector allocations differ from the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), which is a good proxy for its selection universe.

As highlighted in the far right column, FCPI overweights and underweights nearly all sectors by roughly 5%. The overweighting for Real Estate and Utilities is a little low, but that's due to different classification methods. I've categorized securities based on GICS, while Fidelity uses the ICB framework. Either way, you can easily see what FCPI is doing, and as we'll see next, its specific selections within these sectors have strong value and quality features.

In this table, I've also included the First Trust Bloomberg Inflation Sensitive Equity ETF (FTIF), the Avantis Inflation Focused Equity ETF (AVIE), and the ProShares Equities for Rising Rates ETF (EQRR). Based on their sector allocations, they're more aggressive options that could work better in high-inflation environments. However, the consequences of poor timing are significant. As shown below, FCPI has easily outperformed these three peers since April 2023. These results suggest it's a more appropriate long-term hold.

Lastly, please consider FCPI's annual active returns compared to IWB since November 2019. I've highlighted how poorly it did in 2020 when it lagged behind by 18.54%, but given how unusual that year was, I'm not sure of its relevance, though inflation did tick down to just 0.1% in May 2020 as the economy ground to a halt.

Otherwise, the results were pretty solid. FCPI rebounded as inflation increased, especially in 2022, when Energy outperformed, and Technology struggled. It has managed a small active return since 2024.

FCPI Fundamental Analysis

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for FCPI's top 25 holdings, which comprise 64.91% of the portfolio. Notice how at the top of the list are three tech stocks (AAPL, MSFT, NVDA), though their combined weight is about 2.5% less than in IWB. Due to the -5% tilt assigned to this sector, I expect this will always be the case, so it's a little bit of a unique way to avoid too much mega-cap stock concentration.

Keeping in mind that FCPI's Index screens for value, quality, and momentum, here are three additional observations to consider:

FCPI: Substantial Valuation Discount vs. Benchmark

FCPI trades at 15.07x forward earnings, which represents a 24% discount over IWB on a relative basis. It's also deeply discounted using the price-cash flow ratio (10.35x vs. 15.98x) and other value ratios, as summarized below.

FCPI also has a stronger sector-adjusted value score (3.61/10 vs. 2.40/10), but the other three funds also look pretty good from a value perspective. In particular, FTIF is a unique choice, as it's almost entirely composed of stocks in just four sectors (Energy, Industrials, Materials, and Real Estate). FCPI also overweights these Energy and "Real Assets and Infrastructure" sectors, but not nearly as aggressively.

FCPI: Quality Is Outstanding

FCPI's Index screens for stocks with high free cash flow margins, high returns on invested capital, and free cash flow consistency, and based on its 9.46/10 sector-adjusted profit score, derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, the screens work well. In the earlier table, I included FCPI's 25.27% weighted average net margins as one piece of hard evidence, but here is a more detailed look at FCPI's quality features compared to its peers.

FCPI's margins are solid across the board, and it even offers higher historical free cash flow growth than IWB (11.58% vs. 8.96%), which lends to its free cash flow stability screen. FCPI's holdings use more leverage than average, evidenced by its 1.53x weighted average debt-equity ratio, but its debt as a percentage of free cash flow and interest coverage ratios seem fine, so I don't think it's a big issue.

Overall, FCPI's quality is excellent, and, most importantly, it's consistent. I looked back at its composition in July and December 2024, which were different due to its semi-annual reconstitutions, and FCPI's profit score was 9.20/10 and 9.26/10, demonstrating how well the quality screens work.

FCPI: Momentum Advantage Has Dried Up

FCPI's Index evaluates momentum based, in part, on a company's absolute and risk-adjusted twelve-month price returns less one month. The earlier table shows its components have increased in price by 23.30% over the last year, which is about 14% higher than IWB and substantially greater than the three other inflation ETFs. Therefore, I'm satisfied it has good intermediate-term momentum. However, it has less of an advantage over IWB using shorter-term metrics like one-month and six-month price returns, as shown below.

Notice how FCPI's components have declined by 3.36% over the last month compared to 3.30% for IWB, and its -2.60% six-month price return is only marginally better than IWB's -5.25% loss. That means that its holdings were strong performers about 6-12 months ago but haven't really done much since. In addition, now that returns are negative, it seems like any meaningful momentum advantage FCPI had over IWB has dried up, and it might take another Index reconstitution to return.

Investment Recommendation

FCPI hasn't had much interest lately because inflation has steadily declined from its peak in June 2022. However, the average investor might not realize that FCPI only tilts slightly toward inflation-friendly sectors and has robust screens in place to ensure high-quality selections. In addition, its 24% forward P/E ratio discount over IWB makes it an interesting value play, and its -5% active sector allocations to the Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors promote diversification and ensure less reliance on mega-cap growth stocks. High inflation or not, FCPI looks fundamentally sound, so I've decided to reiterate my "buy" rating, and I look forward to continuing the discussion in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.
